oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Eating and rumination behavior in early lactation as predictors of cow resilience
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Scheler, Tessa
Shepley, Elise
Caixeta, Luciano
Florentino, Cainan
Ruch, Megan
Davis, Sara
Filho, Edson
Dorella, Marina
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: A smooth transition into lactation after calving is one the first steps in setting a dairy cow up for a successful lactation. Ad­equate nutrition during this time is an important factor for this transition. Deviations from expected eating and rumination behaviors are potential indicators of a poor transition and early health setbacks, which have the potential to cause immediate and long-term consequences to cow health and performance. Knowing how eating and rumination time relate to these po­tential setbacks could help practitioners in establishing farm protocols that help farm staff identify cows at risk. This study evaluated the association between eating and rumination time in the first week of lactation with a cow’s ability to return to normal performance with no setbacks after calving (resilience).
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 212
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
